question: Explain the core principle of the Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm and describe its key properties.
expectedPoints: BFS explores a graph level by level, starting from the root node.
It uses a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue data structure to manage the nodes to be explored.
It is complete and guarantees to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph.

question: List two advantages and two disadvantages of using the Breadth-First Search algorithm.
expectedPoints: Advantage: It always finds the shortest path solution if one exists (in an unweighted graph).
Advantage: It is complete and will always find a solution if the graph is finite.
Disadvantage: It has high memory consumption as it stores all nodes at the current depth level.
Disadvantage: It can be very slow for large state spaces due to its exponential time complexity.

question: Describe, in detail, the complete step-by-step process of the Breadth-First Search algorithm. Include the data structures used and how they are manipulated.
expectedPoints: Initialization: Start with a root node, mark it as visited, and place it in an empty FIFO queue.
Main Loop: While the queue is not empty, dequeue the front node and examine it. If it is the goal, return it.
Expansion: For each unvisited neighbor of the current node, mark it as visited and enqueue it.
Termination: The algorithm terminates when the queue is empty, meaning all reachable nodes have been explored.
The algorithm produces a breadth-first tree that shows the shortest path from the root to any node.
